main_ingredients: whole fish (sea bass, tilapia, or catfish)

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 600 g including fish, broth, and vegetables)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical communal serving of Szechuan Roast Fish is approximately 600 g, comprising a whole or half fish submerged in spiced mala broth with mixed vegetables, shared between two to three diners.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175168) and Spicy fish dish composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Szechuan Roast Fish as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked fish (sea bass / tilapia), combined with published estimates for Szechuan spiced broth components (chili oil, fermented bean paste, mixed vegetables). Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with the input record values, which are plausible for this dish type. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.5 g/100 g based on the oil-heavy broth composition. Cholesterol estimated at approximately 55 mg/100 g based on fish content. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available at this composite level and are set to -1. Water content not reported at composite level and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Fish and Vegetable Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-carbohydrate fish-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Szechuan Roast Fish. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed fish dishes with vegetables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with low net carbohydrate (approximately 4.5 g/100 g), primarily from fibrous vegetables. Glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated as: (35 × 27 g net carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.45, rounded to 9.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (450 mg/100 g; approximately 2,700 mg per 600 g serving) from pixian doubanjiang, dried chilies, and seasoning sauces
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Szechuan Roast Fish due to its very high sodium load; if consumed, avoid drinking the broth and limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g of fish flesh only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (estimated GI 35), with high protein from fish supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Szechuan Roast Fish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile; however, starchy vegetable additions such as potatoes should be minimised, and the high sodium content requires monitoring in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (8.5 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (450 mg/100 g) from chili oil and fermented pastes, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Szechuan Roast Fish in moderation; the fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, but the high sodium and oil-heavy broth increase cardiovascular risk and the broth should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Szechuan Roast Fish (烤鱼, Kǎoyú) is a bold, communal dish originating from Sichuan Province, China. A whole fish — typically sea bass, tilapia, or catfish — is first marinated, then grilled or roasted until the skin is crispy and the flesh is tender. The cooked fish is placed in a large shallow metal pan and submerged in a richly spiced, numbing-hot broth made from Szechuan peppercorns, dried chilies, pixian doubanjiang (fermented broad bean paste), ginger, garlic, and chili oil. The pan is kept simmering over a portable burner at the table, and an array of vegetables — lotus root, potatoes, bean sprouts, celery, and mushrooms — are added to absorb the intense mala (numbing and spicy) flavours. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 145 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 4.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate option. Sodium is very high at 450 mg/100 g due to fermented pastes and seasonings. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when starchy vegetable additions are minimised. It is not recommended for individuals with hypertension or those on sodium-restricted diets due to its very high sodium content. The fish contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, selenium, vitamin B12, and niacin. Szechuan Roast Fish is a hallmark of mala cuisine and is typically served as a shared main course at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Szechuan Roast Fish as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked fish species and estimated broth/vegetable components. Core macros are plausible and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source directly reports this composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Szechuan Roast Fish as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (55 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked fish and chili oil component data. The input record listed a duplicate portion entry (two identical 600 g entries); only one default serving of 600 g is retained. The input GI source (Sichuan Academy of Agricultural Sciences) is not on the approved GI source list; GI has been re-anchored to a University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 35 × 27 g net carbohydrate per 600 g serving) / 100 = 9.5. This is a Chinese Szechuan dish, not a Singaporean dish; multilingual requirements for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi do not apply under G6.
